name: Loving-kindness (Metta)
shortDescription: Metta is a Pali word meaning loving-kindness, benevolence, or goodwill. It is a central concept in Buddhism, representing the aspiration to bring happiness and benefit to all beings.
description: Metta, often translated as loving-kindness, is a foundational concept in Buddhist philosophy and practice. It represents a selfless and unconditional love, extending without limit to all beings, without discrimination or expectation of return. It is not simply affection or attachment, which can be conditional and based on personal preferences, but a genuine wish for the well-being and happiness of all. Metta is considered one of the four Brahmaviharas (sublime attitudes), along with compassion (karuna), sympathetic joy (mudita), and equanimity (upekkha). Cultivating metta involves developing a deep sense of connection and empathy with others, recognizing our shared humanity and the inherent desire for happiness within each individual. Practices such as metta meditation, where phrases of loving-kindness are directed towards oneself, loved ones, neutral individuals, difficult people, and eventually all beings, are used to cultivate and strengthen this quality of heart. The development of metta is seen as essential for reducing suffering, fostering inner peace, and ultimately progressing on the path to enlightenment. It is closely linked to the Bodhisattva ideal, where practitioners strive to liberate all beings from suffering. The practice of metta helps to break down barriers of prejudice, hatred, and aversion, replacing them with understanding, acceptance, and genuine care. It is a transformative practice that has profound effects on both the individual practitioner and the world around them, promoting harmony, compassion, and a more peaceful existence. Metta is a cornerstone of Buddhist ethics and a vital ingredient for creating a more just and compassionate world.

sources.title: The Heart of the Buddha's Teaching
sources.author: Thich Nhat Hanh
